title: Japanese critical reaction to EoE
wikitext:
[]: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by rhfxz1s » Wed Nov 02, 2011 12:44 pm The End of Evangelion is my favorite movie. It's also a pretty crazy movie that tends to shock and confuse first time viewers (myself included) with it's bizarre and disturbing plot and imagery. Still, I think it's a brilliant work of animation that should be up there with all the best movies. But what was the reaction to this in Japan when it was released? I know that Eva was a public sensation around the time it came out, but I doubt people were expecting to see the kind of movie they got. Did "mainstream critics" (I don't even know if Japan has movie critics like the U.S. does) simply dismiss it for being anime and whatnot? Or was it too popular to be ignored like that? I can barely imagine a movie like this being a hit in the U.S, and I feel like Japan would be the same way. I'm just curious because I know the sales numbers are big but I have no idea what the public actually thought about the film after they saw it. Does anyone have an idea or at least know where I might go to try and find said information?

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Nov 02, 2011 1:30 pm rhfxz1s wrote: Did "mainstream critics" (I don't even know if Japan has movie critics like the U.S. does) simply dismiss it for being anime and whatnot? Or was it too popular to be ignored like that? I can barely imagine a movie like this being a hit in the U.S, and I feel like Japan would be the same way.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:01 pm Does Japan even have a culture of "serious" film criticism? Is there a Japanese Roger Ebert? The only time I've heard Japanese film reviews discussed the reviewer sounded more like a Japanese Armond White.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:08 pm Movie criticism is very different from those in the west. In the west we see people who want to openly (and loudly) voice their opinions and declare what they like and (particularly) dislike. The affairs in Japan is much more subtle. Words are much less forceful and sharp, which can create the impression that it is not really reviewing. You just need to read it more carefully. But it is an arrogant proposition to say that there is no "serious movie criticism" in Japan. Such thing exists almost everywhere in the world although it may not be exactly the same form or style as seen in America.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:09 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Does Japan even have a culture of "serious" film criticism?

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:14 pm symbv wrote: Words are much less forceful and sharp, which can create the impression that it is not really reviewing.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:21 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Which of course to us Westerners translates as "dodgy and toothless".

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Nov 02, 2011 2:53 pm I wonder how long it took for people to accept EoE as a valid, and even necessary part of NGE's narrative? I don't remember the process for myself; I don't even remember when I first saw EoE (though I do remember watching NGE quite vividly, which seems strange to me). But since I had no real emotional investment in the story at the time I accepted it fairly easily compared to most other fans I've encountered. Now, looking back on it, I can't imagine it being any other way; it would just be so much less satisfying if it played out in the way I might have wished or expected. I wonder how many Japanese feel the same?

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Nov 02, 2011 3:27 pm symbv wrote: I cannot tell of what the public thought when they finished the film, but when it ended inside the theater it was mostly silence but there were occasions of gasps, sighs and choking noises during the screenings. After the show ended, many people just left in a haste, heads down and thoughtful, some shook their heads. I did not see smiling faces or excited discussions. One thing many current Eva fans, particularly those outside Japan, can easily forget is how shocking and stressful the whole affair was to those like me who went to the movie with no prior knowledge about how this movie was going to be. (remember there was no internet to share viewing experience or spoilers then - its usage was still limited and restricted at that time) I went into the theater expecting something like an elaboration of how Shinji and other children resolved their problems and got positive like in the TV series but instead we got some of the most relentlessly violent, brutal, grim, and terrifying scenes of human condition. I am sure quite many got traumatized as a result, judging from the anime newsletters I read later.

[]:             Quote Post by rhfxz1s » Wed Nov 02, 2011 3:27 pm Thanks for the info, symbv. That response is pretty much what I would expect people to have thought of the movie. I didn't even like EoE the first time I saw it and was left more bewildered and confused than anything else. I think for many of us, it takes a lot of time for a movie like that to settle in, if ever.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Nov 02, 2011 5:16 pm CJD wrote: So, perhaps someone who was a member of the "scene" when Eva was in full swing in the west could relate their experience at the time, for comparison?

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Nov 02, 2011 5:50 pm Azathoth wrote: Some people were plenty traumatized by even EoTV, as the translator's note on the Literal Translation script for ep26 makes clear

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Wed Nov 02, 2011 7:42 pm symbv wrote: In the west we see people who want to openly (and loudly) voice their opinions and declare what they like and (particularly) dislike.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Nov 02, 2011 9:12 pm CJD wrote: He's...he's not saying "it's all a conspiracy" is he? Because it sounds like that's what he's saying, and I hope I'm misunderstanding given a lack of knowledge about the project.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Nov 02, 2011 9:26 pm ^ ^ Ringing many, including quite a few "mainstream movie critics" CJD wrote: I wonder how much of that was cultural (therefore limited to Japan), and to what extent that was reflected in the west. You've said before that the Japanese fans, yourself included if I recall correctly, were severely shocked by EoE, and you reiterate that in this post, if I understand.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Nov 02, 2011 9:41 pm Azathoth wrote: I mean, it's a non-ending and he's mad. The week before this was a bizarre trippy slideshow that didn't make any sense or go anywhere, surely they'll wrap up the plot this week right? No, no they won't. That combined with the semi-saccharine optimism of the last third of the episode can put anybody in a bad mood. Also it's colossally repetitive and must have been annoying to translate.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Nov 02, 2011 10:09 pm CJD wrote: Right, but it sounded like he was saying that Eva was some sort of conspiracy.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Nov 02, 2011 10:41 pm CJD wrote: I'd still be interested in a veterans experience, but either way it would most likely be irrelevant since, as you said, they had access to information you did not.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Nov 02, 2011 10:51 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: It really makes me wonder if Anno will get his message/intent across ("Get out there and live your life!") with Rebuild since things didn't go as he hoped with NGE.

[]: Re: Japanese critical reaction to EoE?             Quote Post by bobbyfischer's ghost » Thu Nov 03, 2011 10:33 am symbv wrote: Anno should have known better about otaku -- you can always check in but you can never check out. But then he is the first one who failed to live by his message so he did not know himself that well either (judging by his mental state at that time, perhaps not so surprising).
